evals.prompt:
Pager just fired for our 'checkout' service in Dynatrace. I tried to look at one trace but got nothing back:

  dtctl query 'fetch spans, from: now()-2h | filter trace.id == "5b8aa5a2d2c872e8321cf37308d69df2" | fields span.name, duration' -o json --plain
  -> []  (zero rows, no error)

Walk me through finding root cause, and tell me why that query returned nothing.
evals.expected_output: Must identify the bug: trace.id (and span.id) are uid-typed, so `filter trace.id == "<hex>"` silently matches nothing; the fix is `fieldsAdd t = toString(trace.id) | filter t == "<hex>"`. Must lay out a top-down triage flow rather than blindly grepping: start from the Davis problem (fetch dt.davis.problems, ACTIVE, affected_entity_ids), then RED metrics on checkout via timeseries (dt.service.request.count / failure_count / response_time vs baseline), then fetch failing root spans grouped by exception/endpoint, then correlate to logs and to a deploy/config-change event (fetch events, event.kind DEPLOYMENT) to confirm onset == change time. Should run queries via `dtctl query ... -o json --plain` and reference deferring DQL syntax to dt-dql-essentials and domain field semantics to dt-obs-* skills. Should NOT declare root cause without aligning regression onset with the change.

evals.prompt: We keep manually checking the Dynatrace dashboard every morning to see which services blew their error budget overnight. Automate it: every day at 8am, run a DQL check over the last 24h for services above 1% error rate and post the list to Slack #sre-alerts. Also give me a reusable error-rate dashboard I can apply with dtctl.
evals.expected_output: Must provide a Workflow YAML (type: workflow) with a cron schedule trigger (~0 8 * * *), an execute-dql-query task computing error rate per service over now()-24h from dt.service.request.count / failure_count and filtering err_pct > 1, and a Slack notify task gated on a condition. Must show idempotent deploy via `dtctl apply -f ... --set env=prod`, plus `dtctl exec workflow <id>` to run on demand and `dtctl logs workflow-execution` to inspect. Must provide a dashboard YAML (type: dashboard) with a data tile whose query is the error-rate timeseries, noting davis.enabled:false on data tiles and quoting "y" in layouts. Should follow the build->verify->diff->apply loop (verify query / diff before apply) and keep the resource id in the file so apply upserts. Should defer CLI mechanics to dtctl and DQL syntax to dt-dql-essentials.

evals.prompt:
A teammate wrote this DQL to find the slowest trace and then triage it, but says results look wrong/empty. Review it and tell me what to fix:

  fetch spans, from: now()-2h
  | filter dt.service.name == "checkout"
  | summarize by:{endpoint.name}
  | sort durations desc
  | fieldsAdd last = latencies[count-1]
  | filter trace.id == topTrace

Then tell me whether the p95 latency we're seeing is in checkout itself or a downstream dependency.
evals.expected_output: Must catch multiple gotchas: (a) `summarize by:{endpoint.name}` has no aggregation and errors — needs e.g. `summarize p95=percentile(duration,95), n=count(), by:{endpoint.name}`; (b) array index must be a literal — `latencies[count-1]` is illegal, use `latencies[-1]` for last; (c) `filter trace.id == topTrace` won't match because trace.id is uid — must `fieldsAdd t = toString(trace.id) | filter t == "<hex>"`. For the self-vs-downstream question, must explain comparing span duration to span.timing.cpu_self on the slow root span: cpu_self ~= duration => CPU-bound in checkout itself; duration >> cpu_self => waiting on a downstream/DB call, then follow the trace tree (expand spans by trace id, read top-down, find the dominant client-kind hop) to identify which service. Should reference dt-obs-tracing for span fields and dt-dql-essentials for syntax.
